metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that gates the 'teeth' scenario with a concrete pytest filter, e.g. `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_registry.py -k 'teeth or fake or dispatch'`, asserting that `select_backend` returns the registered `FakeBackend` and that reverting raises.
evidence: 'Teeth: via _reset_registry_for_test, register a FakeBackend under a test key, point a Config at it ... and assert select_backend returns the fake — then confirm reverting the registration makes the same lookup raise.'
AC3 proving command is `pytest ... -k 'idempotent or reset'` — the teeth scenario is neither 'idempotent' nor 'reset', so it is excluded from the AC's -k filter and has no AC home.
ABSENCE: no AC checklist item references 'teeth', 'dispatch', 'FakeBackend', or 'selection is honored'.
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test described in the Testing section — registering a FakeBackend, asserting dispatch, then confirming reverting raises — has no corresponding AC checklist item and no proving command in the Acceptance Criteria section. The closest AC (`-k 'idempotent or reset'`) covers state round-trip but not the dispatch assertion itself, leaving the most important behavioral proof (selection is honored, not hard-coded) uncertified.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC3: `-k 'idempotent or reset'`)
scenarios: An executor who implements only idempotency and reset (which AC3 covers) but incorrectly hard-codes JiraBackend in select_backend would satisfy all ACs — because the behavioral dispatch test is described but never gated by an AC.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] `select_backend` dispatches dynamically (honors the registry, not hard-coded): register a `FakeBackend` under a test key via `_reset_registry_for_test`, construct a `Config` with `reconciler.backend` set to that key, assert `select_backend(config)` returns the fake, then assert the same call raises after exit — verified by `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_registry.py -k 'teeth or fake or dispatch'`, exit 0.

findings.finding: The 'Teeth' test's primary behavioral assertion — that `select_backend` returns the registered backend for a *non-default* configured key, not just for 'jira' — is described in the Testing section but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion. AC2 only asserts the default-config/JiraBackend case; AC3 only asserts registry state round-trip. The end-to-end selection correctness for arbitrary keys is untested at the AC level.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' bullet) vs. AC lines 2 and 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] `select_backend(config)` dispatches on the configured key: a config pointing to a non-default registered key returns that key's registered backend (not always JiraBackend) — verified by the Teeth unit test registering a FakeBackend under a test key and asserting it is returned.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND` env-override into an AC with a concrete proving command, e.g. `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira pytest tests/unit/test_config_reconciler_backend.py -k env_override`, exit 0.
evidence: 'REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira env override resolves.' — Testing section only.

AC1 proving command: `pytest tests/unit/test_config_typed.py tests/unit/test_config_reconciler_backend.py` with parenthetical '(valid default resolves + unknown value raises)' — env override not listed as a verified case.
ABSENCE: no AC checklist item mentions REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND or env override.
findings.finding: The `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira` env-override scenario is described in the Testing section but is absent from the Acceptance Criteria checklist — it has no AC and no proving command, so an executor could skip it without failing any AC gate.
findings.location: Testing section (Config-schema unit bullet: 'REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira env override resolves') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC1)
scenarios: An executor who omits the env-variable wiring (e.g. forgets to add REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND to the env layer in _config_sources.py) passes all ACs but silently breaks the advertised env-override feature.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to AC1 (or as a standalone AC):
- [ ] `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira` env override resolves to `reconciler.backend == "jira"` — verified by `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND=jira pytest tests/unit/test_config_reconciler_backend.py -k env_override`, exit 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number annotations in the Edit-set: `ReconcilerConfig` is at `:475` (not `:471`), the `'reconciler'` coercer block is at `:653` (not `:649`), and `jira_cli_timeout` is read at `acli_subprocess.py:61` (not `:71`).
evidence: Plan: 'ReconcilerConfig at src/rebar/_config_schema.py:471'
Plan: 'already consumed by the reconciler at acli_subprocess.py:71 as load_config().reconciler.jira_cli_timeout'
Plan: 'register a constrained coercer for it in the "reconciler" coercer table (the "reconciler": {...} block at :649)'
Actual: ReconcilerConfig starts at line 475; UiConfig occupies lines 466-472 (`:471` is `enabled: bool = False`)
Actual: jira_cli_timeout read is at acli_subprocess.py line 61, not 71
Actual: 'reconciler' coercer dict starts at line 653, not 649
findings.finding: The plan cites `acli_subprocess.py:71` as where `load_config().reconciler.jira_cli_timeout` is consumed, but the actual read is at line 61. Similarly, `ReconcilerConfig` is cited as being at `:471` but it starts at line 475, and the `'reconciler'` coercer block is cited as `:649` but is at line 653. An implementer following these annotations will land in the wrong place (UiConfig, not ReconcilerConfig, is at 471).
findings.location: Edit-set section — `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` bullet; `acli_subprocess.py:71` citation
scenarios: An implementer navigating to ':471' to insert the new `backend` field lands in `UiConfig` and may insert the field in the wrong dataclass.
An implementer navigating to ':649' to add the coercer entry lands in the `mcp` block and may add the entry to the wrong section.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the three citations: `ReconcilerConfig` → `:475`; `"reconciler": {...}` coercer block → `:653`; `acli_subprocess.py` → `:61`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the 'teeth' test constructs a `Config` with a non-`'jira'` `reconciler.backend` value without being rejected by the `_as_choice` coercer — e.g. by constructing `ReconcilerConfig(backend='fake')` directly and passing it into `select_backend()` rather than going through `load_config()`, and update the test description accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'register a FakeBackend under a test key, point a Config at it (reconciler.backend set to that key with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test)'
No mechanism for widening _as_choice at test time is specified in the plan
_as_choice at src/rebar/_config_schema.py:117 raises ConfigError for any value outside the literal set {'jira'} passed at the call site in the coercer table
Config.from_mapping() or load_config() would reject the test-key value before select_backend() is reached
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test requires `reconciler.backend` to be set to a non-'jira' key (e.g. a FakeBackend test key), but the plan offers no mechanism to widen the `_as_choice({"jira"})` guard at test time. A `Config` whose `reconciler.backend` is an unrecognized key would be rejected at config-load by the coercer before `select_backend` is ever called — so the teeth test as described cannot be constructed without bypassing or patching the coercer.
findings.location: Testing section / 'Teeth' paragraph; AC line 3 (_reset_registry_for_test round-trips)
scenarios: Test author constructs a Config with backend='fake_test_key' — the coercer raises ConfigError before select_backend() is called, making the teeth test unreachable as written.
Test author bypasses the coercer by constructing ReconcilerConfig(backend='fake_test_key') directly — but then the AC's env-override test would not exercise the coercer path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Teeth test description, replace 'point a Config at it (reconciler.backend set to that key with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test)' with 'construct `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake_test_key"))` directly (bypassing `_as_choice` validation) and pass it to `select_backend()` — this separates the registry-lookup test from the coercer test, which is already covered by the ConfigError AC item'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism the 'teeth' test uses to get a `Config` with a synthetic backend key past the `_as_choice` guard — e.g., explicitly state that the test constructs `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake_key"))` directly (bypassing `load_config()`), or names a `monkeypatch`-able symbol (a module-level `_ALLOWED_BACKENDS: set[str]`) in `_config_schema.py` that the coercer reads.
evidence: Plan says: 'register a `FakeBackend` under a test key, point a `Config` at it (`reconciler.backend` set to that key with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test)'

The `_as_choice` coercer is a lambda `lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` inlined in the `"reconciler"` coercer dict at `:649-659` — the choice-set `{"jira"}` is a literal, not a module-level variable or injectable.
ABSENCE: No patch target (e.g. `monkeypatch` of the coercer dict, a test-only Config constructor bypass, or a `_ALLOWED_BACKENDS` module-level set) is named anywhere in the plan.
The plan states the config-load guard is 'independent of the registry' — confirming the coercer and registry are separate, but leaving the test-widening mechanism entirely unspecified.
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test requires temporarily widening the `_as_choice` set beyond `{"jira"}` to admit a synthetic test key, but the plan never specifies the mechanism for doing so. The choice-set is a literal `{"jira"}` embedded in a lambda inside `_config_schema.py`'s coercer table — there is no plan-described patch target, parameterization, or bypass that makes `load_config()` accept an unknown backend key in a unit test.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Teeth' paragraph; Edit-set `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` coercer block
scenarios: Without a patch target, the teeth test cannot construct a valid `Config` with `reconciler.backend = "fake_key"` through `load_config()` — it will raise `ConfigError` before `select_backend` is ever called.

An implementer may resort to directly constructing a `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake_key"))` bypassing the coercer entirely — but the plan says 'point a Config at it' and implies using the normal load path.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: the teeth test builds its `Config` by direct dataclass construction (`Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="test_key"), ...)`) rather than via `load_config()`, since the coercer guard is already covered by the config-schema unit tests. `select_backend` receives the `Config` object directly and does not re-validate the key against `_as_choice`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the factory calling convention for `register(key, factory)`: specify whether `factory` is the `Backend` subclass itself (called as `factory(config)` or `factory(settings)` by `select_backend`), a zero-arg callable, or a `Callable[[Config], Backend]` — and show the concrete `@register` usage that makes the equivalence AC (`JiraBackend` built from `resolve_jira_settings()` values) pass.
evidence: Plan writes: 'decorate `JiraBackend` with `@register("jira", …)`' — the `…` is never resolved.

Plan also says `select_backend(config)` 'looks up the factory, and constructs the Backend' — but whether the factory is `JiraBackend` (the class itself as a constructor) or a separate callable that builds it from config fields is unspecified.

The equivalence test requires 'a `Backend` whose `TicketTransport` is an `AcliClient` constructed from the same `resolve_jira_settings()` values' — implying the factory must have access to config context, but the factory signature is undefined.
ABSENCE: No factory signature, call-site, or example instantiation is given in Approach, Edit-set, or Testing.
findings.finding: The `register` decorator's second argument is written as `@register("jira", …)` with a literal ellipsis; the plan never specifies whether that argument is a class (registered directly), a zero-arg factory callable, or a factory that receives a Config object. This is a referenced-but-undefined protocol: an executor cannot write `_backend_registry.py` or `JiraBackend`'s registration without knowing the factory calling convention.
findings.location: Edit-set: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py` — `@register("jira", …)` notation
scenarios: An implementer could write `register(key, cls)` where the registry calls `cls()` with no args — but JiraBackend requires Jira settings to construct its transport.
Alternatively `register(key, factory_fn)` where `factory_fn(config)` is called — but this conflicts with the decorator syntax `@register("jira", …)` which typically decorates the class itself.

The test AC says `select_backend(load_config())` returns a `JiraBackend` built from `resolve_jira_settings()` values — this means either the factory receives the Config, or `select_backend` calls the class with resolved settings — neither path is specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Testing section exactly how the 'teeth' test supplies a non-"jira" backend key — e.g., by constructing `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake"))` directly (bypassing the coercer), then assert `select_backend` uses the registry; clarify that the 'choice-set widening' happens at the dataclass level, not at the config-load coercer level.
evidence: Plan: 'register a FakeBackend under a test key, point a Config at it (reconciler.backend set to that key with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test)'
Plan: 'the static choice-set ({"jira"} today) is the config-load guard; the registry below does the authoritative runtime object lookup'
_config_schema.py:653–659 shows the reconciler coercer table uses a lambda closure — the choice-set is a literal baked into the lambda at module-load time; there is no public API to widen it
The `_as_choice` function at `:117` is a closed function; the coercer is `lambda v, k: _as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` — an immutable closure
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test as described requires `load_config()` (or equivalent) to accept a non-"jira" backend value, but the static `_as_choice` coercer is baked into `_SECTIONS["reconciler"]["backend"]` as a closure over the literal set `{"jira"}` — there is no specified mechanism to temporarily widen it in a test. The plan says 'the choice-set temporarily widened in the test' but names no mechanism, leaving the implementer to either discover that `Config` must be constructed directly (bypassing the coercer) or incorrectly attempt to patch the baked-in lambda.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Teeth' paragraph; also Acceptance Criteria AC #3 (idempotent/reset test)
scenarios: Implementer calls `load_config()` with `[reconciler] backend = "fake"` in a test config — the coercer raises `ConfigError` before the registry is consulted, making the teeth test impossible as written
Implementer patches the coercer lambda in `_SECTIONS` — fragile, couples the test to schema internals and may break other tests
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ambiguous 'choice-set temporarily widened' phrase with: 'Construct a `Config` object directly (e.g. `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake"))`) to bypass the `_as_choice` coercer — `select_backend` only reads `config.reconciler.backend`, so this is the clean test seam. The `_reset_registry_for_test` context manager handles registry isolation; no coercer patching is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the teeth-test mechanism: state that the test should construct a `Config` (or `ReconcilerConfig`) dataclass directly with `backend='fake_key'` — bypassing `load_config()` and the `_as_choice` coercer — rather than patching the coercer or env layer.
evidence: 'with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test' — mechanism left to executor.

The plan explicitly says the static choice-set is 'the config-load guard' and the registry does 'the authoritative runtime object lookup', implying they are separate — but how to bypass the guard in a test is not stated.
findings.finding: The 'teeth' test specifies that the choice-set must be 'temporarily widened in the test' to allow a non-production key like 'fake_key', but the mechanism for doing so is unspecified — the executor must independently decide whether to construct a `Config` dataclass directly (bypassing `load_config()`/the coercer), monkey-patch `_as_choice`, or use another approach.
findings.location: Testing section, 'Teeth' bullet: 'point a Config at it (reconciler.backend set to that key with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test)'
scenarios: An executor who patches `_as_choice` globally could break other tests; one who constructs `Config` directly bypasses a different code path than `load_config()`. The right approach has non-obvious trade-offs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing > Teeth bullet, append: '(construct a `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake_key", ...))` directly — no need to patch the `_as_choice` coercer since the coercer is only invoked by `load_config()`)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or a runtime assertion in `select_backend`) that validates the `_as_choice` allowed-set matches the registry's registered keys, OR document the two-place update requirement explicitly in `_backend_registry.py` with a comment pointing to the `_as_choice` location that must be kept in sync.
evidence: Plan states: 'A second backend adds its key to both the `_as_choice` set and the registry' — two-place update, no sync mechanism
The choice-set `{"jira"}` in `_config_schema.py` is structurally separate from the registry dict in `_backend_registry.py`; neither references the other
ABSENCE: no test asserts that the `_as_choice` set equals the registry's registered keys
findings.finding: Adding a second backend requires updating two separate locations — the `_as_choice({"jira"})` set in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` AND the registry in `_backend_registry.py` — with no mechanism to keep them in sync; a developer who registers a new key but forgets the coercer (or vice versa) gets a silent partial failure.
findings.location: Context/Edit-set: '`_as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})` pattern' and 'A second backend adds its key to both the `_as_choice` set and the registry'
scenarios: Developer adds `LinearBackend` and registers it in the registry but forgets to extend `_as_choice` — config-load rejects the key with a misleading 'must be one of: jira' error
Developer extends `_as_choice` to accept 'linear' but forgets to register the factory — config load passes, `select_backend` raises a registry KeyError at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend_registry.py`, expose a `registered_keys()` function; in `_config_schema.py`, derive the `_as_choice` set from `registered_keys()` (with a lazy import guarded by the same lazy-import pattern already used in `select_backend`). This collapses the two-place update to one. Alternatively, add a test in `test_backend_registry.py` that asserts `set(registered_keys()) == _RECONCILER_BACKEND_CHOICE_SET` to catch drift at CI time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Testing section (Teeth paragraph) exactly how the test constructs a `Config` with `backend='fake'` — either state that direct `Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend='fake'))` construction is used (bypassing the coercer, no 'widening' needed) or describe an explicit patching/parameterization mechanism for the `_as_choice` set.
evidence: Testing: 'with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test'
Approach: 'select_backend ... lazily imports rebar_reconciler.adapters inside the function' and 'An unknown key (one that somehow bypassed the _as_choice load-time guard) raises a registry error'
Edit-set: '_as_choice(v, k, {"jira"})' described as providing 'config-load static validation' — a load-time, not runtime, guard
No mechanism for widening the choice-set is described in Approach or Edit-set
findings.finding: The Testing section describes the 'teeth' test using a 'FakeBackend' under a test key by pointing a Config at it 'with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test', but neither the Approach nor the Edit-set describes any mechanism for widening the _as_choice set at test time. _as_choice is defined in _config_schema.py as a config-load-time validator; the Approach positions it as a static, baked-in guard. An implementer reading both sections is sent in two directions: the Approach implies _as_choice is static/immutable, while Testing implies it is dynamically adjustable per-test. The plan should explicitly state how the test constructs or bypasses the Config (e.g., direct dataclass construction bypasses the coercer) or describe a patching mechanism.
findings.location: Testing section (Teeth paragraph) vs. Approach section (_as_choice description) vs. Edit-set (_config_schema.py coercer)
scenarios: Implementer writes the teeth test and finds they cannot call load_config() with backend='fake' because _as_choice rejects it at config-load; they must either construct Config directly (bypassing the coercer) or monkey-patch _config_schema.py — neither approach is specified, causing rework or inconsistent test implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'with the choice-set temporarily widened in the test' with 'by constructing Config(reconciler=ReconcilerConfig(backend="fake")) directly (bypassing load_config() and its _as_choice coercer)' — since direct dataclass construction is the natural way to inject an out-of-band key in a unit test without patching the schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either include the one-line ADR 0035 update (noting `reconciler.backend` is config-driven via `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND`) in this story's edit-set, or add an explicit AC gate (e.g., a linked ADR PR) so the doc lands atomically with the code.
evidence: Plan explicitly defers ADR 0035 update to a follow-up story rather than including it here
REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND env override is operator-facing and auto-derived but not documented in this story's deliverables
The backend registry pattern (decorator + lazy import trigger) is a novel architectural decision with no ADR capture in scope
findings.finding: The plan defers the ADR 0035 / design-doc update for the new `reconciler.backend` config key and backend-registry pattern to a future story, meaning the code ships with an undocumented operator-facing env var (`REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND`) and a new architectural pattern (decorator registry + lazy-import trigger) with no doc trail in this story.
findings.location: Scope section: 'a doc note in ADR 0035 that selection is config-driven (a one-line S1/ADR follow-up, tracked there, not blocking here)'
scenarios: An operator reading the config reference finds no mention of REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND between story merge and the ADR follow-up landing
A future contributor adds a second backend following the pattern but cannot find the registry contract documented, leading to inconsistent implementation
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/docs/adr/0035-*.md` (or equivalent ADR file) to the edit-set with a one-paragraph note covering: the `reconciler.backend` config key, its `_as_choice` validation, the `REBAR_RECONCILER_BACKEND` env override, and the decorator-registry + lazy-import-trigger pattern — matching the level of detail already present in the plan's Approach section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit threading.RLock (or equivalent) guarding all mutations to the registry dict (register, _reset_registry_for_test) and document that the lock is held across the read-check-then-write in `register`, so the 'conflicting factory raises' invariant is race-free.
evidence: 'register is idempotent (same key+factory = no-op; conflicting factory raises)' — implies a read-check-then-write with no atomicity guarantee
'_reset_registry_for_test() context manager' — explicitly mutates the registry dict outside Python import lock
No mention of a lock, RLock, or copy-on-write swap in the plan
findings.finding: The module-level registry dict is mutated by `_reset_registry_for_test` (and could be mutated by tests registering fake backends) outside of Python's import lock, with no explicit lock or atomic swap described. The 'conflicting factory raises' check is a read-then-write that is not atomic, creating a TOCTOU race if two test threads or coroutines call register/reset concurrently.
findings.location: Approach section — '_reset_registry_for_test' and 'register is idempotent' description; Testing section — 'Teeth' paragraph
scenarios: Parallel pytest workers (same process, e.g. pytest-xdist threads) both call _reset_registry_for_test and register simultaneously, causing one to see stale state or skip the 'conflicting factory' raise
A future caller registers a backend at runtime (not import-time), racing with select_backend's lazy import
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend_registry.py`, add `_registry_lock = threading.RLock()` and wrap the body of `register` and `_reset_registry_for_test` with `with _registry_lock:` to make read-check-write atomic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured debug/info log in `select_backend` recording which backend key was resolved and constructed, and ensure any registry-lookup or construction exception is logged with context (key, registered keys) before re-raising.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, or tracing anywhere in the plan for this code path
'an unknown key … raises a registry error naming the registered keys' — error is raised but not logged
OBSERVABILITY sub-criterion of T5b: 'are new failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert so operators can see and debug them?'
findings.finding: The plan does not specify observability (logging or metrics) for the `select_backend` call path — neither a debug/info log on successful backend construction nor an error log on failure — so operators have no signal when the wrong backend is selected or when backend construction silently fails after the config-load guard is bypassed.
findings.location: Approach section — 'select_backend(config) reads config.reconciler.backend, looks up the factory, and constructs the Backend'
scenarios: A misconfigured deployment silently falls back to an unexpected backend (or fails opaquely) with no log entry to guide diagnosis
Backend construction raises (e.g., resolve_jira_settings() can't find credentials) and the only signal is an unhandled exception traceback with no structured context

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in the plan (and a matching comment in `_backend_registry.py`) that `select_backend()` is intentionally dead production code until S4 (rebar:6673-7636-a116-4f90) merges, and confirm that the dead-code lint contract (`test_module_size_contract.py` or equivalent) does not flag exported-but-uncalled symbols in infrastructure modules.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'No consumer is rewired here — select_backend is proven equivalent to today's construction in isolation; S4 changes the call sites.'
AC bullet 4: 'No existing consumer … is modified' — confirmed 0 production callers in this story.
Codebase grep for 'select_backend': no matches in src/rebar — confirming the function does not yet exist and will land as dead code until S4 merges.
Pattern precedent: existing registries (STEP_REGISTRY, _GATES) are imported and invoked at construction time within the same story that introduces them — not deferred to a subsequent story.
findings.finding: `select_backend()` is introduced with zero production call sites in this story and the plan contains no guard (comment, lint suppression, or AC assertion) to prevent dead-code tooling or a future refactor from pruning it before S4 ships.
findings.location: Scope section / AC bullet 2 (`select_backend` verified by registry unit test only)
scenarios: A linter or dead-code scanner (e.g. vulture) flags `select_backend` as unused and a future cleanup PR removes it before S4 is merged, breaking S4's dependency.
A reviewer of the S3 PR questions whether the function is tested-in-isolation-only infrastructure or genuinely needed, and requests deferring it to S4 — requiring a plan revision mid-sprint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the edit-set that `adapters/jira/backend.py` is an **edit** to the S2-created file (adding `@register`), not a new file creation, so the implementer expects a non-empty diff on a pre-existing file.
evidence: Plan: 'decorate JiraBackend with @register("jira", …) (the JiraBackend class itself is introduced by S2, a prerequisite; this story only adds its registration)'
S2 ticket ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 status: 'open', manifest: 'dep absent src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py'
AC git-diff guard lists 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py' as an allowed modified path — correctly acknowledging S3 touches this file
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py` as the target for `@register("jira", …)` decoration, but that file is created by the S2 prerequisite (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) which is still 'open' with 'dep absent' in its manifest. The plan does not address what happens if S2 is merged without the `@register` decoration already present — specifically, it does not state whether S3 adds the decorator to an already-written file, or whether S2 is expected to include it. If S2 ships without the decorator, S3's edit to `adapters/jira/backend.py` is an in-place modification of an S2 artifact, which may create a merge conflict or implementation ambiguity.
findings.location: Edit-set / 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py' bullet
scenarios: S2 ships a complete JiraBackend without a @register call; S3 implementer must add the decorator as an edit to an existing file — this is correctly scoped but worth acknowledging explicitly so the implementer knows to expect a non-empty diff on that file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the two-case idempotency rule ('same key+factory = no-op via identity check; different factory for same key raises `RegistryError` naming the key') into the Approach or Edit-set description of `_backend_registry.py`, so it is co-located with the module specification an implementer reads first.
evidence: Approach says only: 'The registry is a module-level dict populated by an idempotent `register` decorator' — 'idempotent' is named but the two-case rule is not defined here.
Testing says: '`register` is idempotent (same key+factory = no-op; a conflicting factory for the same key raises)' — this is where the actual decision rule lives.
AC line 3 references the same rule: 'same key+factory = no-op; conflicting factory raises' — still in the AC, not the module spec.

ABSENCE: The Edit-set description of `_backend_registry.py` lists `register`, `select_backend`, and `_reset_registry_for_test` but gives no decision rule for what 'idempotent' means in the conflicting-factory case.
findings.finding: The idempotency and conflicting-registration decision rules ('same key+factory = no-op; a conflicting factory for the same key raises') are stated only in the Testing section, not in the Approach or Edit-set description of `_backend_registry.py`. This is a processing protocol that must be implemented inside the registry module but is not co-located with the module's specification.
findings.location: Approach section (`_backend_registry.py` description) vs. Testing section ('register is idempotent')
scenarios: An implementer reading only the Approach/Edit-set could implement `register` as always-overwrite (last-write wins) rather than raise-on-conflict — this would silently swallow a wiring bug in production.

The 'same key+factory = no-op' check requires identity comparison on the factory (is it `factory is existing_factory` or `factory == existing_factory`?) — this is unspecified and affects the test outcome.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Edit-set bullet for `_backend_registry.py`: '`register(key, factory)` stores `factory` under `key`; if `key` is already present and `existing is factory` (identity), it is a no-op; if `existing is not factory`, it raises a `RegistryError` naming the key and both factories.'

findings.finding: The plan states that `select_backend` ensures the registry is populated 'via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter before resolving'. The actual `adapters/__init__.py` contains only comments — it has zero import statements and does not import any adapter sub-package. The described auto-population mechanism does not exist and must be built as part of this story.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Registration happens at import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`; `select_backend` imports that module (via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving, so the registry is populated deterministically regardless of import order.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py` to the edit-set with a concrete change: `from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.adapters import jira as _jira_adapter  # noqa: F401 — triggers @register side-effect` (or equivalent), and include a test asserting the registry is non-empty after importing `_backend_registry`.

findings.finding: The plan claims `select_backend` populates the registry deterministically 'via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter,' but `adapters/__init__.py` is a comment-only file with zero import statements. The import-triggering behavior the plan relies on does not exist, and modifying `adapters/__init__.py` is absent from the edit-set.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Registration happens at import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`; `select_backend` imports that module (via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `adapters/__init__.py` to the edit-set with `from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira import backend as _jira_backend  # noqa: F401 — triggers registration`, or (b) have `select_backend` in `_backend_registry.py` explicitly execute `import rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.backend` before the registry lookup, making the registration self-contained without relying on an outside import.

findings.finding: The AC directs running `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config.py` to verify the `ReconcilerConfig.backend` field, but that file tests the ENGINE-side `rebar_reconciler/config.py` (flat domain constants: `EXCLUDED_FIELDS`, status maps) — not the app-level `ReconcilerConfig` in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`. Additionally, the AC also names `tests/unit/test_config_schema.py`, which does not exist anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, first bullet: `pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config.py tests/unit/test_config_schema.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add the `ReconcilerConfig.backend` cases to `tests/unit/test_config_backcompat.py` (which already tests `ReconcilerConfig` field additions/removals) and update the AC to reference that file. Remove the reference to the non-existent `tests/unit/test_config_schema.py`.

findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for deterministic adapter registration relies on 'the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter', but the actual adapters/__init__.py (src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py) contains only comments — it has no import statements and does not import any sub-adapter. If select_backend calls into or imports via this file to populate the registry before resolving, the registry will be empty at lookup time and every select_backend call will fail with a registry error.
findings.location: Approach section: 'select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_backend_registry.py`, make `select_backend` explicitly trigger registration by importing the adapters package that actually imports its sub-adapters: update `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py` to add `from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira import backend as _jira_backend  # noqa: F401` so that importing `adapters` (which `_backend_registry` does before resolving) deterministically registers JiraBackend. Also note this file path must be added to the AC4 git-diff allowlist.

findings.finding: The plan states that `select_backend` ensures the registry is populated by relying on 'the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter.' The actual `adapters/__init__.py` (verified) contains only a docstring — it does NOT import any adapter sub-packages. The claimed import-chain guarantee for deterministic registry population does not exist in the current codebase.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Registration happens at import of `adapters/jira/backend.py`; `select_backend` imports that module (via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter)...'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py` to the Edit-set with the change: add `from . import jira.backend  # noqa: F401  (registers JiraBackend)` so the import-chain guarantee is real, not assumed.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
